stories.headline: Craig Morton, Broncos Ring of Famer Who Led Denver to First Super Bowl, Dies at 83
stories.summary:
Craig Morton, the quarterback who led the Denver Broncos to their first Super Bowl appearance in the 1977 season, died Saturday at his home in Mill Valley, California. He was 83. The Broncos organization confirmed the news Monday after Morton's family notified the team.

Morton joined the Broncos in 1977 at age 34, guiding the team to a 12-2 record and earning the NFL's Comeback Player of the Year award. He quarterbacked the Orange Crush-era Broncos through an AFC Championship victory over the Oakland Raiders before falling to the Dallas Cowboys in Super Bowl XII. Morton finished his six seasons in Denver with a 41-23 record as a starter and 11,895 passing yards.

Morton made history as the first quarterback to lead two different franchises to the Super Bowl, having taken the Cowboys to Super Bowl VI in 1970. He played 18 NFL seasons in 207 career games. He was inducted into the Broncos Ring of Fame in 1988 and the College Football Hall of Fame in 1992.

"He was our leader that year that we went 12-2, the first year he came to Denver," said fellow Ring of Famer and former safety Steve Foley. "It was a magical season. He was just tough as nails."

stories.headline: Update: Avalanche Win Game 4 Over Wild 5-2, Lead Series 3-1 Heading Into Home Game 5
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche defeated the Minnesota Wild 5-2 in Game 4 of the Western Conference Second Round on Monday at Grand Casino Arena in St. Paul, taking a commanding 3-1 series lead. Parker Kelly scored his first career Stanley Cup Playoff goal in the third period to break a 2-2 tie, and Mackenzie Blackwood made 19 saves in his first start of the postseason.

Nazem Kadri added a power-play goal and Martin Necas contributed two assists for Colorado. Blackwood, who had not started since April 14, provided a steady presence after the Avalanche lost Game 3 by a 5-1 score on Saturday.

"For me, the biggest change from last game to this game is we were a more determined group," Avalanche coach Jared Bednar said. "To a man, just more determined and committed to playing the right way."

Colorado can advance to the Western Conference Final with a victory in Game 5 at Ball Arena in Denver on Wednesday at 8 p.m. ET on HBO Max, truTV and TNT.

stories.headline: Wildfire Near Carter Lake in Loveland Prompts Mandatory Evacuations for Sedona Hills Area
stories.summary:
A wildfire burning northeast of Carter Lake in Larimer County prompted mandatory evacuation orders Monday for residents in the Sedona Hills area near Loveland. The fire was estimated at approximately 16 to 30 acres, according to multiple updates from the Colorado Division of Fire Prevention and Control and the Larimer Emergency Telephone Authority.

Aerial resources dispatched to the scene included a multi-mission aircraft, two fixed-wing single-engine airtankers and a helicopter. Additional ground resources were drawn from agencies across the Front Range to assist with containment efforts.

Evacuation orders covered the area stretching from where North Colorado Road 29 meets West Eisenhower Boulevard south to the Carter Lake vicinity. Residents in Sedona Hills were ordered to leave immediately as crews worked to establish containment lines.

stories.headline: Suncor Refinery in Commerce City Spews Black and Yellow Smoke After Power Outage Forces Shutdown
stories.summary:
Thick black and yellow smoke billowed from two smokestacks at the Suncor Energy refinery in Commerce City on Monday after a power outage caused all three production plants to shut down. The outage was triggered when an Xcel Energy transmission line tripped offline, cutting power to the facility and forcing an emergency restart of operations.

Suncor issued a community notification at 1:06 p.m. stating that increased smoke and flaring might be visible and that no emergency action was necessary. The company said its air monitoring network did not detect toxic fumes and that residents and workers should not have experienced acute health problems. The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment's Air Pollution Control Division dispatched an inspector to the site.

State air division meteorologists said wind conditions on Monday likely lifted pollutants away from the ground, though officials said they would continue monitoring as Suncor files mandatory reports. The incident drew renewed criticism from community advocates and elected officials, including Adams County Commissioner Steve O'Dorisio, who lives near the refinery and has long demanded greater accountability from the company.

"You can say it's an error, a mistake, an accident," O'Dorisio said. "You know what also isn't good? To say this is routine." Cultivando, a public health advocacy group in Adams County, also criticized the emergency notification system for providing few details as smoke poured into the air.

stories.headline: Denver Pays $225,000 Settlement After Officers Wrongfully Held Couple at Gunpoint and Fired at Them
stories.summary:
The Denver City Council approved a $225,000 settlement Monday for two people whose car was mistaken for a suspect's vehicle by Denver police officers in May 2024. Officers Kirk Malone, Brayan Silva and Brian Camozzi approached the wrong car with guns drawn and Officer Malone accidentally discharged his weapon, striking a nearby patio umbrella.

Jessica Sjostrom and Rex Hickman were driving a maroon Mercury south on Brighton Boulevard when officers — acting on information from the HALO camera network — incorrectly identified their vehicle as a red Saturn linked to a gun threat. The actual suspect's car was a different type, color and license plate, and was heading in a different direction.

Sjostrom and Hickman were pulled from their car, handcuffed and searched before officers realized the error. They say officers continued to treat them as suspects for hours after the mistaken stop, according to a 9News interview. The case was scheduled for trial in April but the parties reached a settlement beforehand.

Councilwoman Shontel Lewis noted that the payout brings Denver's total police settlement costs to approximately $784,000 so far in 2026.

stories.headline: Gun Magazine Found on Frontier Airlines Flight at DIA Believed to Belong to Law Enforcement Officer
stories.summary:
A Frontier Airlines flight bound for Phoenix was delayed Sunday evening at Denver International Airport after a gun ammunition magazine was discovered on board as the aircraft prepared for departure. The plane was scheduled to leave at 8:08 p.m., but passengers were deplaned and rescreened as a precaution, and the aircraft underwent a security sweep that found no additional items.

Frontier spokesperson Rob Harris said investigators believe the magazine was left on the plane by a law enforcement officer on a previous flight. Neither the airline nor airport officials disclosed where on the plane the magazine was found or how it bypassed TSA screening. Gun parts including magazines are prohibited in carry-on baggage under TSA rules but may be transported in checked bags.

The delay caused the flight crew to exceed their maximum duty time, forcing all passengers to be rebooked on an early Monday morning flight that departed DIA just before 6 a.m. and landed in Arizona shortly before 7 a.m. The incident is Frontier's second high-profile security event at DIA in less than a week, coming days after a pedestrian was struck and killed by a departing Frontier aircraft on a runway.

stories.headline: Colorado Legislature Advances Trio of AI Bills Targeting Health Insurance, Psychotherapy and Chatbots
stories.summary:
With just days remaining in the 2026 legislative session, Colorado lawmakers advanced three artificial intelligence bills aimed at regulating the technology's use in health care, mental health therapy and consumer-facing chatbots. The measures are heading to Gov. Jared Polis for his signature.

House Bill 1139 would prohibit health insurance companies from basing coverage decisions solely on AI-generated group data, requiring systems to also consider individual patient medical histories. The bill passed the House 47-15. A second measure, House Bill 1195, would bar therapists and social workers from using AI to make treatment recommendations without clinician review and require patient consent before using AI to record therapy sessions.

Sponsors cited growing data showing that one in eight patients between ages 12 and 21 uses AI chatbots for mental health advice, and that a third of adults would consider consulting a chatbot rather than a therapist. Rep. Javier Mabrey, D-Denver, noted that chatbots are designed to keep users engaged rather than challenge them — a concern he called especially dangerous in mental health crises.

The three bills are separate from a previously reported rewrite of Colorado's landmark AI algorithmic discrimination law, which also cleared the legislature over the weekend and is awaiting Polis' signature.

stories.headline: Colorado Lawmakers Approve Rewrite of Landmark AI Algorithmic Discrimination Law
stories.summary:
Colorado state lawmakers approved sweeping changes to the state's 2024 artificial intelligence law over the weekend, sending the rewrite to Gov. Jared Polis, who indicated he plans to sign it. Senate Bill 26-189 establishes a new accountability framework for AI systems used to make consequential decisions about employment, housing, loans, insurance and health care.

The updated law requires companies to provide consumers with clear notice when automated decision-making technology is used and gives individuals the right to request human review of adverse decisions made by algorithms. It replaces the previous requirement for risk assessments with a framework clarifying who bears responsibility when someone is harmed by algorithmic discrimination, a change aimed at relieving concerns from businesses about liability for technology they did not develop.

Beginning January 1, 2027, AI developers must supply companies with descriptions of the technology's intended uses, data categories used for training, known limitations and risks, and guidance on appropriate human review. The Attorney General would enforce the law through the Colorado Consumer Protection Act.

A Polis spokesperson called the framework "a nation-leading model." Labor groups including Colorado's AFL-CIO chapter said the bill is a good first step but fell short by not requiring pre-deployment testing to ensure systems do not discriminate.

stories.headline: Denver Public Schools Asks Judge to Dismiss Racketeering Lawsuit Over Lease-Finance Debt Structure
stories.summary:
Denver Public Schools filed a motion Monday asking a judge to dismiss a racketeering lawsuit brought by parent advocacy group Mamás de DPS, which alleges the district used a lease-finance structure to unlawfully conceal debt from voters. The lawsuit challenges the district's use of Certificates of Participation, or COPs, arguing the arrangement effectively mortgages school buildings without voter approval in violation of the Colorado Constitution.

The complaint alleges DPS created a "shell corporation" — the Denver Schools Leasing Corp. — that takes nominal ownership of school buildings, often for as little as $10, then uses those buildings as collateral to raise money through COP sales. Investors are repaid through lease payments from the district. Because COPs are not backed by the district's full faith and credit, they typically carry higher interest rates than voter-approved bonds.

The lawsuit also contends the district misled voters into approving billions in bond debt while using some proceeds for impermissible purposes, including general fund relief and COP debt repayment. The Denver Gazette previously reported that approximately $25 million from the 2020 bond was spent on general fund relief, and a district presentation showed $11 million earmarked to retire COP debt associated with Kepner Beacon Middle School.

District attorneys argue the plaintiff lacks standing, that the Colorado Organized Crime Control Act claim is barred by governmental immunity, and that the claim is implausible on its face. District spokesperson Scott Pribble declined to comment, citing the ongoing litigation.

stories.headline: East Colfax Rapid Bus Construction Crosses Into Aurora, Beginning 18 Months of New Road Work
stories.summary:
The $280 million East Colfax bus rapid transit project expanded into Aurora this week for the first time, launching approximately 18 months of construction along the busy corridor east of the Denver city line. The project aims to reduce bus travel times by 15 to 30 minutes along the full route, with the new rapid bus system expected to launch by the end of 2027.

The Aurora segment will use a different design than the Denver portion: rather than dedicated center bus lanes, buses in Aurora will travel along the sides of Colfax and merge with general traffic. Project officials say this approach should result in less disruption than the Denver construction, though they acknowledged challenges for nearby businesses.

Several Denver businesses along the corridor have already closed or relocated due to the first phase of construction, which began in the fall of 2024. Cerebral Brewing co-owner Sean Buchan said the Denver work cut 20 percent from his bottom line and that he now faces a second round of disruption at his Aurora location at Colfax and Florence Street.

"We saw people who would no longer drive to get here," Buchan said of the Denver phase. "We saw a drastic year-over-year reduction in business." Denver's Department of Transportation and Infrastructure, which is overseeing the full project, said all business access will be maintained throughout construction, though some temporary pedestrian detours may be needed.

stories.headline: RTD Warns of Bus and Light Rail Detours, Delays for 20th Annual Colfax Marathon This Sunday
stories.summary:
The Regional Transportation District is warning riders to expect detours and delays on multiple bus and light rail lines this Sunday, May 17, as the 20th anniversary Colfax Marathon moves through Denver and Lakewood. Service disruptions will run from 5:30 a.m. until approximately noon.

The D and H light rail lines will bypass Colfax at Auraria Station and several downtown stops, rerouting through Auraria West, Empower Field at Mile High, Ball Arena-Elitch Gardens and Union Station. The L Line will not operate at all during the event; RTD advises those riders to use bus Route 43 as an alternative.

Dozen of bus routes — including the 0, 1, 6, 8, 9, 10, 12, 15, 15L, 16, 19, 20 and 24, among others — will detour as needed but will cross the race course when safe. RTD noted that scheduled connections could be affected and advised riders to use the agency's online trip planner before heading out. A separate minor detour for routes 20 and 24 is also planned Saturday from 9 a.m. to noon for the Colfax 5K.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis Meets With Mesa County Business Leaders to Discuss Opportunity Zone Investments for Rural Colorado
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis traveled to Grand Junction on Tuesday to meet with economic development leaders from Mesa County and the Western Slope, focusing on how to attract private investment through the federal Opportunity Zone program. The meeting at Colorado Mesa University included representatives from the Office of Economic Development and International Trade, the Grand Junction Economic Partnership and the Business Incubator Center.

The discussion centered on the program's new "OZ 2.0" iteration, created under federal legislation signed in July 2025, which made the program permanent after its original sunset date of December 2028. The U.S. Treasury released a list of eligible census tracts on April 6, and Colorado is seeking public input on which tracts to nominate by June 30, 2026. The governor can nominate up to 25 percent of the state's low-income census tracts.

Polis noted that Colorado's existing opportunity zones have attracted investment in 75 percent of designated areas, with 60 percent located in rural parts of the state. He said the nomination process requires careful input from local communities to ensure designations match development potential.

"We wish that we could designate them all. Unfortunately, we could only designate about one out of every four," Polis said. "So it's really hearing from people on the ground" to determine which tracts offer the greatest opportunities for housing, jobs and long-term economic growth.

weather.notable: About 14 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: Pop quiz: What's the plural of moose? Moosen. I saw a flock of moosen.

topStory.headline: Trump Rejects Iran Ceasefire Counterproposal, Calls Talks 'Totally Unacceptable' as Strait of Hormuz Closure Persists
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump on Sunday rejected Iran's latest counterproposal to end the two-month-old war in the Middle East, declaring the offer 'totally unacceptable' and accusing Tehran of 'playing games.' Trump described the ceasefire as being on 'life support' and claimed Iran had backed away from an earlier agreement that would have allowed the United States to help extract its highly enriched uranium. Iran's proposal was transmitted through mediator Pakistan.

Iran's counterproposal, as reported by Iranian media and people familiar with its contents, called for a halt to hostilities on all fronts — including in Lebanon, where Israel has conducted airstrikes and a ground invasion — before addressing broader issues such as Tehran's nuclear program and support for regional proxy groups. On the question of Iran's estimated 440 kilograms of highly enriched uranium, Tehran proposed diluting some of the stockpile and transferring the remainder to a third country, while offering to suspend enrichment for a shorter period than the 20-year moratorium the United States demanded. Iran rejected any dismantling of its nuclear facilities outright.

The core impasse centers on sequencing. Tehran wants hostilities to end before nuclear negotiations begin, while Trump has insisted that Iran must first reopen the Strait of Hormuz — a critical waterway through which roughly one-fifth of global oil and natural gas exports move during peacetime — and address its nuclear capabilities as a precondition. Iran's de facto closure of the strait followed U.S. and Israeli strikes on the country beginning February 28, and a continued U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports has further complicated ceasefire efforts. Both sides have continued to attack, capture, and intercept ships in the region.

Trump is traveling to Beijing to meet with Chinese President Xi Jinping, partly in hopes of enlisting China's leverage over Iran to advance a resolution. China is the world's largest buyer of Iranian oil and has been working diplomatically through Pakistan to help broker a peace agreement. However, White House officials have set low expectations for a breakthrough on Iran specifically, expressing concern that the dispute not derail broader U.S.-China negotiations on trade and fentanyl precursor exports.

On the ground, the conflict continues to expand beyond Iran's borders. Israel has deployed Iron Dome anti-missile batteries and personnel to the United Arab Emirates, a move acknowledged by U.S. Ambassador Mike Huckabee at a Tel Aviv conference, reflecting growing defense cooperation among Abraham Accords nations wary of Iran. Israeli strikes in southern Lebanon killed six people and wounded seven others on Tuesday, despite a ceasefire agreement there. With talks stalled and the Strait of Hormuz still closed, analysts and officials see little near-term path to a durable settlement.
